en.extract:
The yellow-browed sparrow (Ammodramus aurifrons) is a species of bird in the family Passerellidae.  First described by Johann Baptist von Spix in 1825, this American sparrow is found across much of the Amazon basin in South America. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist shrubland, pastureland, and heavily degraded former forest.


Taxonomy
When Johann Baptist von Spix first described the yellow-browed sparrow in 1825, he put it in the now-defunct genus Tanagra, believing it to be a tanager. The classification error was soon recognized, and the species was moved first to the genus Ammodramus, then to Myospiza—a genus Robert Ridgway created in 1898 for this and the closely related grassland sparrow. Most taxonomists now subsume Myospiza into Ammodramus. DNA analysis indicates that the yellow-browed sparrow is a sister species to the grassland sparrow, and that these two species make a sister group with the grasshopper sparrow; these three are genetically distinct from the other Ammodramus sparrows. The yellow-browed sparrow has four subspecies, which differ primarily in the extent of yellow on the face and the amount of streaking on the upperparts and crown:

A. a. apurensis, first described by William Henry Phelps and Ernest Thomas Gilliard in 1941, is found in northeastern Colombia.
A. a. cherriei, first described by Frank Chapman in 1914, is found in central Colombia.
A. a. tenebrosus, first described by John Todd Zimmer and Phelps in 1949, ranges from southeastern Colombia through southwestern Venezuela into the adjacent areas of Brazil.
A. a. aurifrons, described by Spix in 1825, ranges south from southeastern Colombia into central Bolivia and east along the Amazon basin to the Atlantic coast.
The yellow-browed sparrow is one of nine sparrows in the genus Ammodramus, a name which means "desert runner" or "desert racer" (from the Greek ammos, meaning "desert" and -dromos, meaning "-racer" or "-runner").  The species name aurifrons is a combination of the Latin words auri, meaning "gold" and frons, meaning "forehead" or "front".


Description
The yellow-browed sparrow is a small American sparrow, measuring 13 cm (5 in) in length, with a mass between 14.5 and 19 g (0.5 and 0.7 oz). Sexes are similarly plumaged, though males average very slightly larger than females. The adult is brownish gray on the upperparts, with dusky streaks on the back. Its underparts are whitish, shading to grayish on the chest and buff on the flanks. Its legs are pinkish-brown, while the beak is horn-colored, with a darker culmen. Its iris is reddish brown. The juvenile, which has no yellow on its face or wings, is buffier than adults are, with thin brownish streaks on its breast and flanks.


Similar species
Although quite similar to the grassland sparrow, the yellow-browed sparrow typically shows more yellow on its face. It is overall paler and less streaked than the grassland sparrow, and lacks chestnut edges to its flight feathers. It is best told by its buzzy song, its less secretive behavior and less restrictive habitat preferences.


Habitat and range
Although its former habitat was largely along rivers and on islands, the yellow-browed sparrow is now widespread in grassy areas of the Amazon biome, including agricultural fields, roadsides and around towns. It is generally found in the lowlands, though it has been recorded to 1,000 m (3,281 ft) on the east side of the Andes, and regularly as high as 1,500 m (4,921 ft) — and sometimes to 2,000 m (6,562 ft) — in Ecuador.


Behavior
The yellow-browed sparrow is generally easier to see than its sister species is. It is less secretive, and is found in a wider range of grasslands.


Food and feeding
Like most American sparrows, the yellow-browed sparrow is largely granivorous, feeding on the seeds of native grasses and various introduced plants. It also eats insects, including larvae. It typically feeds on the ground.


Breeding
Breeding has been recorded between February and September. The yellow-browed sparrow builds a cup nest of dried grass lined with fine plant material. The nest is generally on the ground in a grass clump, though sometimes it is placed low in a shrub. The female lays 2–3 white eggs.


Voice
The yellow-browed sparrow's high-pitched song is described as "insect-like". Monotonous and buzzy, it is transcribed as tic, tzzz-tzzzzz, with the first note weak and short. The male sings throughout the day from a low, exposed perch.


Conservation and threats
Because of its very large range and apparently stable population, the yellow-browed sparrow has been designated by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) as a species of least concern. It is described as common throughout its range, though its numbers have not been quantified. This species has benefited from the clearing of rainforest for agriculture and cattle ranching; it is common in early successional fields.


References


Cited works
Jobling, James A. (2010). The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Names. London, UK: Christopher Helm. ISBN 978-1-4081-2501-4.
Ridgely, Robert S.; Tudor, Guy (1989). The Birds of South America, volume 1: The Oscine Passerines. Austin, TX, US: University of Texas Press. ISBN 978-0-292-70756-6.


External links

Yellow-browed sparrow photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)
"Yellow-browed sparrow media". Internet Bird Collection.
Audio recordings of Yellow-browed sparrow on Xeno-canto.

[[File:Ammodramus aurifrons - Yellow-browed Sparrow XC242089.mp3|thumb|Song of Yellow-browed sparrow ]]
The '''yellow-browed sparrow''' ('''''Ammodramus aurifrons''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Passerellidae]].  First described by [[Johann Baptist von Spix]] in 1825, this [[American sparrow]] is found across much of the [[Amazon basin]] in South America. Its natural [[habitat]]s are subtropical or tropical moist [[shrubland]], pastureland, and heavily degraded former forest.

==Taxonomy==
When [[Johann Baptist von Spix]] first described the yellow-browed sparrow in 1825, he put it in the now-defunct genus ''Tanagra'', believing it to be a tanager.<ref name = "Byers"/> The classification error was soon recognized, and the species was moved first to the genus ''Ammodramus'', then to ''Myospiza''—a genus [[Robert Ridgway]] created in 1898 for this and the closely related [[grassland sparrow]].<ref name = "Dickerman"/> Most [[taxonomist]]s now [[wiktionary:subsume|subsume]] ''Myospiza'' into ''Ammodramus''.<ref name = "Ridgley2-465"/> [[DNA analysis]] indicates that the yellow-browed sparrow is a [[sister species]] to the grassland sparrow, and that these two species make a [[sister group]] with the [[grasshopper sparrow]]; these three are genetically distinct from the other ''Ammodramus'' sparrows.<ref name = "Zink"/> The yellow-browed sparrow has four subspecies, which differ primarily in the extent of yellow on the face and the amount of streaking on the upperparts and crown:<ref name = "Byers"/>
*''A. a. apurensis'', first described by [[William Henry Phelps]] and [[Ernest Thomas Gilliard]] in 1941,<ref name = "ITIS"/> is found in northeastern [[Colombia]].<ref name = "Byers"/>
*''A. a. cherriei'', first described by [[Frank Chapman (ornithologist)|Frank Chapman]] in 1914,<ref name = "ITIS"/> is found in central [[Colombia]].<ref name = "Byers"/>
*''A. a. tenebrosus'', first described by [[John Todd Zimmer]] and Phelps in 1949,<ref name = "ITIS"/> ranges from southeastern Colombia through southwestern Venezuela into the adjacent areas of Brazil.<ref name = "Byers"/>
*''A. a. aurifrons'', described by Spix in 1825,<ref name = "ITIS"/> ranges south from southeastern Colombia into central Bolivia and east along the Amazon basin to the Atlantic coast.<ref name = "Byers"/>

The yellow-browed sparrow is one of nine sparrows in the genus ''Ammodramus'', a name which means "desert runner" or "desert racer" (from the [[Greek language|Greek]] ''ammos'', meaning "desert" and ''-dromos'', meaning "-racer" or "-runner").<ref>Jobling (2010), p. 45.</ref>  The [[specific name (zoology)|species name]] ''aurifrons'' is a combination of the [[Latin]] words ''auri'', meaning "gold" and ''frons'', meaning "forehead" or "front".<ref>Jobling (2010), p. 62.</ref>

==Description==
The yellow-browed sparrow is a small [[American sparrow]], measuring {{convert|13|cm|in|abbr=on|0}} in length,<ref name = "Ridgely"/> with a mass between {{convert|14.5|and|19|g|oz|abbr=on|1}}.<ref name = "Dunning"/> Sexes are similarly [[plumage]]d, though males average very slightly larger than females.<ref name = "Byers"/> The adult is brownish gray on the upperparts, with dusky streaks on the back.<ref name = "Ridgely"/> Its underparts are whitish, shading to grayish on the chest and buff on the flanks. Its legs are pinkish-brown, while the beak is horn-colored, with a darker [[culmen (bird)|culmen]]. Its [[iris (anatomy)|iris]] is reddish brown.<ref name = "Byers"/> The juvenile, which has no yellow on its face or wings, is buffier than adults are, with thin brownish streaks on its breast and flanks.<ref name = "Byers"/>

===Similar species===
Although quite similar to the grassland sparrow, the yellow-browed sparrow typically shows more yellow on its face. It is overall paler and less streaked than the grassland sparrow, and lacks chestnut edges to its [[flight feather]]s. It is best told by its buzzy song, its less secretive behavior and less restrictive habitat preferences.<ref name = "Ridgely2-466"/>

==Habitat and range==
Although its former habitat was largely along rivers and on islands, the yellow-browed sparrow is now widespread in grassy areas of the [[Amazon biome]], including agricultural fields, roadsides and around towns.<ref name = "Ridgely"/> It is generally found in the lowlands, though it has been recorded to {{convert|1000|m|ft|abbr=on|0}} on the east side of the [[Andes]], and regularly as high as {{convert|1500|m|ft|abbr=on|0}} — and sometimes to {{convert|2000|m|ft|abbr=on|0}} — in Ecuador.<ref name = "Byers"/>

==Behavior==
The yellow-browed sparrow is generally easier to see than its sister species is. It is less secretive, and is found in a wider range of grasslands.<ref name = "Byers"/>

===Food and feeding===
Like most American sparrows, the yellow-browed sparrow is largely [[granivorous]], feeding on the seeds of native grasses and various introduced plants.<ref name = "Oren"/> It also eats insects, including [[larvae]].<ref name= "Byers"/> It typically feeds on the ground.<ref name = "Ridgely"/>

===Breeding===
Breeding has been recorded between February and September. The yellow-browed sparrow builds a [[bird nest#Cup|cup nest]] of dried grass lined with fine plant material. The nest is generally on the ground in a grass clump, though sometimes it is placed low in a shrub. The female lays 2–3 white [[Bird egg|eggs]].<ref name = "Byers"/>

===Voice===
The yellow-browed sparrow's high-pitched [[bird song|song]] is described as "insect-like".<ref name = "Byers"/> Monotonous and buzzy, it is transcribed as ''tic, tzzz-tzzzzz'',<ref name = "Ridgely2-466"/> with the first note weak and short.<ref name = "Byers"/> The male sings throughout the day from a low, exposed perch.<ref name = "Byers"/><ref name = "Ridgely2-466"/>

==Conservation and threats==
Because of its very large range and apparently stable population, the yellow-browed sparrow has been designated by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN) as a species of [[least concern]]. It is described as common throughout its range, though its numbers have not been quantified.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> This species has benefited from the clearing of [[rainforest]] for agriculture and cattle ranching; it is common in [[ecological succession|early successional]] fields.<ref name = "McKay"/>
